event: Paleolithic and later stone tools and rock shelters in the hills and Kathmandu Valley indicate early human habitation.
Early Tibeto-Burman and other peoples settle the Himalayan valleys and form agricultural communities.
The Kirat (Kirati) dynasty, traditionally led by Yalamber, establishes early rule over the Kathmandu Valley.
Siddhartha Gautama, the future Buddha, is born at Lumbini in the Shakya republic of present-day southern Nepal.
Emperor Ashoka of the Maurya Empire visits Lumbini and erects a commemorative pillar marking the Buddha's birthplace.
King Manadeva I of the Licchavi dynasty issues the Changu Narayan inscription, the earliest dated inscription in Nepal.
Amshuvarma rises to power in the Licchavi kingdom, strengthening trade ties with Tibet and India.
A Licchavi princess (traditionally Bhrikuti) is said to marry Tibetan king Songtsen Gampo, helping spread Buddhism northward.
The Nepal Sambat calendar era begins, later used widely by the Newar people of the Kathmandu Valley.
Arideva (Ari Malla) founds the Malla dynasty, ushering in a long era of art, architecture, and Newari culture.
Sultan Shams ud-Din Ilyas of Bengal raids and sacks the Kathmandu Valley, damaging temples and shrines.
Jayasthiti Malla consolidates power and codifies social and legal structures across the Kathmandu Valley.
Yaksha Malla expands and consolidates the Malla kingdom to its greatest territorial extent.
After Yaksha Malla's death, the kingdom fragments into the rival city-states of Kathmandu, Patan, and Bhaktapur.
Dravya Shah founds the principality of Gorkha, the future seat of the Shah dynasty that would unify Nepal.
Prithvi Narayan Shah ascends the throne of Gorkha and begins his campaign to unify the kingdoms of the region.
Prithvi Narayan Shah conquers Kathmandu, founding the unified Kingdom of Nepal under the Shah dynasty.
Shah forces complete the conquest of the Kathmandu Valley, capturing Bhaktapur and ending Malla rule.
Nepal launches military campaigns into Tibet, beginning a series of conflicts over trade and territory.
Qing China intervenes on Tibet's behalf, forcing Nepal to sue for peace after the Sino-Nepalese War.
The Anglo-Nepalese War (Gurkha War) breaks out as Nepal clashes with the British East India Company over territory.
The Treaty of Sugauli ends the war, ceding territory to Britain and establishing Nepal's enduring borders.
Jung Bahadur Rana seizes power in the Kot Massacre, founding the hereditary Rana prime ministership.
Nepal sends Gurkha troops to aid Britain in suppressing the Indian Rebellion, deepening Anglo-Nepali ties.
Britain recognizes Nepal's full sovereignty and independence through a treaty of friendship.
A massive earthquake devastates the Kathmandu Valley and eastern Nepal, killing thousands and destroying monuments.
A revolution backed by King Tribhuvan ends 104 years of autocratic Rana rule and restores the monarchy's power.
Tenzing Norgay, a Nepali Sherpa, and Edmund Hillary make the first confirmed summit of Mount Everest.
Nepal is admitted to the United Nations as a member state.
Nepal holds its first democratic general elections, bringing the Nepali Congress to power under B.P. Koirala.
King Mahendra dissolves parliament, bans political parties, and assumes direct rule.
King Mahendra introduces the partyless Panchayat system under a new constitution centralizing royal power.
The Jana Andolan (People's Movement) forces King Birendra to end the Panchayat system and accept multiparty democracy.
The Communist Party of Nepal (Maoist) launches a decade-long armed insurgency known as the People's War.
King Birendra and most of the royal family are killed in a palace massacre, and Gyanendra becomes king.
King Gyanendra dismisses the government and assumes direct authoritarian rule, suspending civil liberties.
The Jana Andolan II protests force Gyanendra to restore parliament, and a peace accord ends the Maoist war.
Nepal's Constituent Assembly abolishes the monarchy and declares the country a federal democratic republic.
A magnitude-7.8 earthquake kills nearly 9,000 people, and a new federal constitution is promulgated.
Nepal holds its first elections under the new federal constitution, establishing provincial and federal governments.
Nepal updates its official map to assert claims over the Kalapani, Lipulekh, and Limpiyadhura border areas with India.
Pushpa Kamal Dahal 'Prachanda' leads a coalition government amid ongoing political realignment in the federal republic.
